topicArea: Gifts, Obligation and the Fiction of Pure Generosity

questions.passage:
A gift is often praised because it appears to escape calculation. Unlike a purchase, it is not supposed to close an account. It arrives wrapped in a claim of freedom: I chose this for you; nothing is owed. Yet everyone knows that gifts create ledgers of a peculiar kind. A birthday forgotten, a wedding present too cheap, a thank-you note delayed, or a favor returned too quickly can all disturb the fragile fiction that generosity floats above exchange.

The fiction matters because it protects the gift from becoming a contract. If every present announced its expected return, gratitude would harden into debt. But the fiction also hides power. The giver may insist that nothing is owed while silently expecting admiration, loyalty, forgiveness, or access. The receiver may feel trapped precisely because the obligation cannot be named. A loan can be negotiated; a gift that demands repayment while denying that it demands anything is harder to refuse.

This ambiguity is not a corruption of gift-giving but part of its structure. Gifts live between market exchange and pure benevolence. They carry social meaning because they are neither wholly priced nor wholly free. A book given by a friend is not valuable only because of its cover price, nor only because of the friend's affection. It matters because object, occasion, relationship, and memory become entangled. To reduce the gift to economics is crude; to deny its obligations is naive.

Modern advice often tries to purify giving by recommending experiences over objects, anonymous donations over public generosity, or spontaneous presents over ceremonial ones. Such advice can be useful when it loosens competition and display. But it can also misunderstand ritual. A ceremonial gift is not necessarily less sincere because it is expected. The expectation may be what allows people to enact care when private emotion is awkward, uncertain, or insufficiently articulate. Ritual can cheapen feeling, but it can also carry feeling when feeling alone would be mute.

The ambiguity becomes sharper when generosity crosses unequal relationships. A powerful person can give in ways that feel gracious to outsiders and coercive to recipients, while a modest gift among equals may carry no such burden. The author is therefore less interested in whether a gift has a market value than in how it rearranges freedom. A tactful gift leaves the receiver able to respond without surrendering dignity. A manipulative gift turns gratitude into a corridor with only one acceptable exit.

The author is also alert to the way gifts can pretend to be speechless while saying a great deal. A lavish present may apologize without admitting fault; a practical present may express intimacy more accurately than a romantic one; an anonymous donation may free the recipient from gratitude while depriving the giver of recognition. None of these meanings can be read from the object alone. The gift's moral force lies in the relationship it enters and the freedom it leaves behind.

The better ethic of gifts is not purity but tact. Tact recognizes obligation without making it oppressive. It allows gratitude without demanding surrender. It understands that the best gifts do not abolish social bonds; they handle those bonds gracefully. A gift should neither pretend to be outside relationship nor reduce relationship to repayment. Its success lies in sustaining the delicate space where indebtedness can be felt without becoming imprisonment.
questions.stem: Which statement best captures the passage's central argument about gifts?

choices.A: The passage argues that gifts are merely disguised purchases and should be treated as contracts.
choices.B: The passage claims that true generosity requires anonymous giving in every circumstance.
choices.C: Gifts are meaningful because they sit between freedom and obligation, and their ethics depend on whether they preserve the recipient's dignity.
choices.D: The passage treats obligation as a corruption that sincere gifts must eliminate.

explanations.A: The author says reducing gifts to economics is crude. Gifts are neither wholly priced nor wholly free.  This reading would make the essay's position cleaner than it is. The author instead uses the examples to keep gifts under judgment rather than simply accepting or rejecting it.
explanations.B: Anonymous giving is mentioned as modern advice, but the author says such advice can misunderstand ritual and recognition.  A student might choose this by noticing a nearby term in the passage, but the relation among the ideas is different: the author is testing limits, not announcing pure freedom, contract, or coercive gratitude.
explanations.C: Correct. The answer captures the central tension: gifts create obligations without becoming contracts, and the moral question is whether those obligations become coercive. This fits the passage because its argument repeatedly turns on obligation handled with tact and dignity, not on a one-sided rule about gifts.
explanations.D: The author argues that ambiguity and obligation are part of the gift's structure, not accidental corruptions.  The passage leaves room for a narrower version of this thought, but not for the broader conclusion stated here; its emphasis remains on obligation handled with tact and dignity.

questions.stem: The examples of thank-you notes, cheap presents, and forgotten birthdays primarily show that:

choices.A: ritual gifts are less sincere than spontaneous gifts because they follow social rules.
choices.B: economic value is the only reliable measure of whether a gift is appropriate.
choices.C: modern etiquette has made gift-giving too complicated to have moral meaning.
choices.D: gift-giving creates social ledgers even when everyone pretends no account is being kept.

explanations.A: The passage later argues that ritual can carry feeling when emotion alone would be mute.  A student might choose this by noticing a nearby term in the passage, but the relation among the ideas is different: the author is testing limits, not announcing pure freedom, contract, or coercive gratitude.
explanations.B: The examples include price, timing, and response, showing that gifts involve more than market value.  The option misses the passage's central movement from usefulness to qualification. It treats one part of the discussion as though it cancelled the rest.
explanations.C: The author sees complication as part of gift structure, not evidence that meaning has disappeared.  The passage leaves room for a narrower version of this thought, but not for the broader conclusion stated here; its emphasis remains on obligation handled with tact and dignity.
explanations.D: Correct. The opening examples reveal the hidden accounting inside a practice that publicly claims to escape calculation. The wording also preserves the essay's characteristic balance: it recognizes a real value while marking the point at which that value can mislead.

questions.stem: The passage most strongly implies that unnamed obligation can be especially troubling because it:

choices.A: is difficult to negotiate or refuse when the giver denies that repayment is expected.
choices.B: is always stronger than a legal debt because it has no written terms.
choices.C: prevents recipients from feeling any genuine gratitude.
choices.D: makes ceremonial gifts impossible to distinguish from manipulation.

explanations.A: Correct. The passage notes that a loan can be negotiated, while a gift that demands repayment while denying demand is harder to refuse. The trouble lies in concealed expectation. That balance is crucial, since the author generally resists both outright rejection and uncritical approval.
explanations.B: The passage compares gifts and loans but does not claim informal obligation is always stronger. The issue is its denial and ambiguity.  The passage leaves room for a narrower version of this thought, but not for the broader conclusion stated here; its emphasis remains on obligation handled with tact and dignity.
explanations.C: The passage allows gratitude; it objects when gratitude becomes a corridor with only one acceptable exit.  This reading would make the essay's position cleaner than it is. The author instead uses the examples to keep gifts under judgment rather than simply accepting or rejecting it.
explanations.D: The author explicitly says ceremonial gifts can carry care. Expectation is not automatically coercion.  A student might choose this by noticing a nearby term in the passage, but the relation among the ideas is different: the author is testing limits, not announcing pure freedom, contract, or coercive gratitude.

questions.stem: Which gift would the author most likely call tactful?

choices.A: A present that acknowledges a relationship without requiring the recipient to repay with loyalty, admiration, or access.
choices.B: A lavish favor from a supervisor that is described as freely given but clearly expects personal allegiance.
choices.C: A gift whose only value is its market price, since price makes obligation measurable.
choices.D: A spontaneous present that ignores the recipient's circumstances because sincerity matters more than social meaning.

explanations.A: Correct. Tact does not abolish obligation; it handles obligation without making the recipient surrender dignity or freedom. This fits the passage because its argument repeatedly turns on obligation handled with tact and dignity, not on a one-sided rule about gifts.
explanations.B: This is the manipulative form the passage criticizes: gratitude becomes coercive because the expected return cannot be openly negotiated.  This reading would make the essay's position cleaner than it is. The author instead uses the examples to keep gifts under judgment rather than simply accepting or rejecting it.
explanations.C: The passage rejects reducing gifts to economics. Price alone cannot capture object, occasion, relationship, and memory.  A student might choose this by noticing a nearby term in the passage, but the relation among the ideas is different: the author is testing limits, not announcing pure freedom, contract, or coercive gratitude.
explanations.D: The author would resist separating sincerity from the relationship a gift enters and the freedom it leaves behind.  The option misses the passage's central movement from usefulness to qualification. It treats one part of the discussion as though it cancelled the rest.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward ritualized gift-giving is:

choices.A: dismissive, because expected gifts are never sincere.
choices.B: qualified approval, since ritual can express care even though it can also become competitive or hollow.
choices.C: uncritical, because ceremonies automatically protect gifts from power and manipulation.
choices.D: irrelevant, because the passage treats only private gifts between close friends.

explanations.A: The passage says expectation may allow people to enact care when private emotion is awkward or insufficiently articulate.  This reading would make the essay's position cleaner than it is. The author instead uses the examples to keep gifts under judgment rather than simply accepting or rejecting it.
explanations.B: Correct. The author resists the modern impulse to purify giving by eliminating ritual. Ritual may cheapen feeling, but it can also make feeling socially available. The wording also preserves the essay's characteristic balance: it recognizes a real value while marking the point at which that value can mislead.
explanations.C: The author says ritual can cheapen feeling as well as carry it. The approval is qualified.  The option misses the passage's central movement from usefulness to qualification. It treats one part of the discussion as though it cancelled the rest.
explanations.D: The passage discusses weddings, ceremonies, anonymous donations, and unequal relationships, not only intimate private exchange.  The passage leaves room for a narrower version of this thought, but not for the broader conclusion stated here; its emphasis remains on obligation handled with tact and dignity.

questions.stem: Which claim would most weaken the passage's argument?

choices.A: Some people prefer experiences to objects when receiving presents.
choices.B: Public donors sometimes give anonymously to avoid praise.
choices.C: Recipients report feeling freer when expectations surrounding a gift are openly acknowledged rather than denied.
choices.D: A book from a friend can matter for reasons beyond its cover price.

explanations.A: This supports one modern preference but does not challenge the passage's deeper claim about obligation and dignity.  A student might choose this by noticing a nearby term in the passage, but the relation among the ideas is different: the author is testing limits, not announcing pure freedom, contract, or coercive gratitude.
explanations.B: The passage already allows that anonymous donation can loosen certain obligations. It does not undermine the structural ambiguity of gifts.  The option misses the passage's central movement from usefulness to qualification. It treats one part of the discussion as though it cancelled the rest.
explanations.C: Correct. This finding would not destroy the whole argument, but it would challenge the idea that unnamed obligation is necessary to protect gifts from contract-like accounting. Open acknowledgment might preserve freedom better. That balance is crucial, since the author generally resists both outright rejection and uncritical approval.
explanations.D: This reinforces the passage's claim that gifts cannot be reduced to economics.  This reading would make the essay's position cleaner than it is. The author instead uses the examples to keep gifts under judgment rather than simply accepting or rejecting it.

questions.stem: The final paragraph suggests that the best gifts sustain:

choices.A: a fiction that nothing at all is owed between giver and receiver.
choices.B: a relationship in which indebtedness can be felt without becoming coercive repayment.
choices.C: a clear contract so neither side has to interpret social meaning.
choices.D: a permanent hierarchy in which the giver's generosity entitles him to gratitude.

explanations.A: The passage treats that fiction as protective but incomplete. The final paragraph recognizes obligation rather than denying it.  The option misses the passage's central movement from usefulness to qualification. It treats one part of the discussion as though it cancelled the rest.
explanations.B: Correct. The final paragraph defines tact as a way to keep obligation humane. A successful gift allows gratitude without imprisonment. This fits the passage because its argument repeatedly turns on obligation handled with tact and dignity, not on a one-sided rule about gifts.
explanations.C: The author does not want gifts reduced to contracts. The delicate space between relation and repayment matters.  This reading would make the essay's position cleaner than it is. The author instead uses the examples to keep gifts under judgment rather than simply accepting or rejecting it.
explanations.D: This is the manipulative corridor the passage criticizes, not the ethic it endorses.  A student might choose this by noticing a nearby term in the passage, but the relation among the ideas is different: the author is testing limits, not announcing pure freedom, contract, or coercive gratitude.
